info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Continuous Probability Distributions

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), then \(P(\mu-\sigma<X<\mu+\sigma)\approx\):

feedback.text: Correct. For a normal distribution, approximately 68.27% of the probability lies within 1 standard deviation of the mean.
Incorrect. 95.45% lies within 2 standard deviations.
Incorrect. Only 50% lies on either side of the mean, but 68.27% lies within 1 standard deviation.
Incorrect. 99.73% lies within 3 standard deviations.

questiontext.text: For \(X\sim\text{Exp}(\lambda)\) with density \(f(x)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) (\(x>0\)), the median is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(F(m)=1/2\Rightarrow 1-e^{-\lambda m}=1/2\Rightarrow e^{-\lambda m}=1/2\Rightarrow m=\ln2/\lambda\).
Incorrect. \(1/\lambda\) is the mean of the exponential distribution; the median is \(\ln2/\lambda\approx0.693/\lambda\).
Incorrect. The median is \(\ln2/\lambda\), not \(2/\lambda\).
Incorrect. The median is \(\ln2/\lambda\approx0.693/\lambda\neq0.5/\lambda\).

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\Gamma(\alpha,\beta)\) with density \(f(x)\propto x^{\alpha-1}e^{-x/\beta}\), then the mean and variance are:

feedback.text: Correct. For Gamma\((\alpha,\beta)\) with scale \(\beta\): mean \(=\alpha\beta\) and variance \(=\alpha\beta^2\).
Incorrect. These formulas apply when \(\beta\) is the rate parameter (not the scale).
Incorrect. Mean \(=\alpha\beta\) and Var \(=\alpha\beta^2\).
Incorrect. Mean and variance of Gamma\((\alpha,\beta)\) are \(\alpha\beta\) and \(\alpha\beta^2\).

questiontext.text: For \(X\sim\text{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)\) on \([0,1]\), the mean is \(\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}\). For a symmetric Beta with \(\alpha=\beta=2\), the variance is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(\text{Var}=\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}=\frac{4}{16\times5}=\frac{4}{80}=\frac{1}{20}\).
Incorrect. \(1/12\) is the variance of Uniform\((0,1)\) (Beta\((1,1)\)), not Beta\((2,2)\).
Incorrect. \(\text{Var}=\frac{2\times2}{4^2\times5}=\frac{4}{80}=1/20\).
Incorrect. The variance of Beta\((2,2)\) is \(1/20\).

questiontext.text: If \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) are i.i.d. \(N(0,1)\), then \(\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2\sim\chi^2(n)\). The mean and variance of \(\chi^2(n)\) are:

feedback.text: Correct. \(\chi^2(n)=\Gamma(n/2,2)\), so mean \(=(n/2)\times2=n\) and variance \(=(n/2)\times4=2n\).
Incorrect. Mean of \(\chi^2(n)\) is \(n\) (not \(n/2\)) and variance is \(2n\).
Incorrect. Variance of \(\chi^2(n)\) is \(2n\), not \(n^2\).
Incorrect. \(\chi^2\) is non-negative; mean \(=n>0\).

questiontext.text:
If \(Z\sim N(0,1)\) and \(V\sim\chi^2(
u)\) are independent, then \(T=Z/\sqrt{V/
u}\) follows a \(t\)-distribution with \(
u\) degrees of freedom. The variance of \(T\) is:

feedback.text:
Correct. The variance of \(t(
u)\) is \(
u/(
u-2)\) for \(
u>2\). For \(
u\leq2\), the variance is infinite.
Incorrect. Variance \(=1\) only as \(\nu\to\infty\) (when \(t\to N(0,1)\)).

Incorrect. Variance of \(t(
u)\) is \(
u/(
u-2)\), not \(
u\).

Incorrect. Variance \(=
u/(
u-2)\), not \(1/(
u-2)\).

questiontext.text: If \(U\sim\chi^2(m)\) and \(V\sim\chi^2(n)\) are independent, then \(F=\frac{U/m}{V/n}\sim F(m,n)\). If \(T\sim t(n)\), then \(T^2\sim\):

feedback.text: Correct. \(T=Z/\sqrt{V/n}\), so \(T^2=Z^2/(V/n)\sim\chi^2(1)/\chi^2(n)/n\cdot n=\chi^2(1)/1\div\chi^2(n)/n=F(1,n)\).
Incorrect. \(T^2\sim F(1,n)\), not \(F(n,1)\). Note \(F(1,n)\neq F(n,1)\).
Incorrect. \(T^2\sim F(1,n)\), which is related to but distinct from \(\chi^2\).
Incorrect. \(T^2\sim F(1,n)\), a well-known result.

questiontext.text: If \(Y=e^X\) where \(X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), then \(Y\) is log-normal. The mean of \(Y\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(E[Y]=E[e^X]=M_X(1)=e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}\) (using the MGF of the normal distribution).
Incorrect. Jensen's inequality: \(E[e^X]>e^{E[X]}=e^\mu\) (strict inequality since \(e^x\) is strictly convex). Correct mean is \(e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}\).
Incorrect. \(\mu=E[X]=E[\ln Y]\), not \(E[Y]\).
Incorrect. Mean of log-normal is \(e^{\mu+\sigma^2/2}\), not \(e^{\mu+\sigma^2}\).

questiontext.text: The Pareto distribution with shape \(\alpha>0\) and scale \(x_m>0\) has CDF \(F(x)=1-(x_m/x)^\alpha\) for \(x\geq x_m\). The mean exists iff \(\alpha>1\) and equals:

feedback.text: Correct. \(E[X]=\int_{x_m}^\infty x\cdot\frac{\alpha x_m^\alpha}{x^{\alpha+1}}\,dx=\frac{\alpha x_m}{\alpha-1}\) for \(\alpha>1\).
Incorrect. Mean of Pareto is \(\alpha x_m/(\alpha-1)\).
Incorrect. Mean is \(\alpha x_m/(\alpha-1)\neq\alpha x_m\).
Incorrect. Mean of Pareto is \(\alpha x_m/(\alpha-1)\).

questiontext.text: The standard Cauchy distribution has density \(f(x)=\frac{1}{\pi(1+x^2)}\). Which statement is true?

answer.text: The mean does not exist (the integral \(\int_{-\infty}^\infty x f(x)\,dx\) diverges)

feedback.text: Correct. The Cauchy distribution has no finite moments of any positive order. The integral for the mean diverges (the tails are too heavy).
Incorrect. While the density is symmetric, the mean does not exist as a Lebesgue integral (the positive and negative parts both diverge).
Incorrect. The Cauchy mean does not exist.
Incorrect. The Cauchy distribution has no finite variance (nor finite mean).

questiontext.text: For \(n\) i.i.d. Uniform\((0,1)\) random variables, the distribution of the \(k\)-th order statistic \(X_{(k)}\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. For Uniform\((0,1)\) samples, \(X_{(k)}\sim\text{Beta}(k,n-k+1)\) with mean \(k/(n+1)\).
Incorrect. Order statistics of uniform samples follow Beta distributions, not Uniform.
Incorrect. The \(k\)-th order statistic is Beta\((k, n-k+1)\), not Beta\((n-k,k+1)\).
Incorrect. The parameters are integers \(k\) and \(n-k+1\), not fractions.

questiontext.text: If \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) are i.i.d. \(N(\mu,\sigma^2)\), then \(\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum X_i\) follows:

feedback.text: Correct. The sample mean of i.i.d. normals is normal: \(\bar{X}\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)\).
Incorrect. The variance of \(\bar{X}\) is \(\sigma^2/n\), not \(\sigma^2\).
Incorrect. That is the distribution of \(\sum X_i\), not \(\bar{X}\).
Incorrect. The sample mean is normal; \(\chi^2(n)\) relates to the sum of squared standard normals.

questiontext.text: The Weibull distribution with shape \(k>0\) and scale \(\lambda>0\) has CDF \(F(x)=1-e^{-(x/\lambda)^k}\). When \(k=1\), this reduces to:

feedback.text: Correct. Weibull\((1,\lambda)\) is Exp\((1/\lambda)\): \(F(x)=1-e^{-x/\lambda}\), the exponential CDF.
Incorrect. Weibull with \(k=1\) gives exponential, not normal.
Incorrect. Weibull\((1,\lambda)\) is exponential with mean \(\lambda\).
Incorrect. \(k=1\) gives the exponential (Gamma\((1,\lambda)\)), not Gamma\((2,\lambda)\).

questiontext.text: If \(X\sim\text{Exp}(1)\), the distribution of \(Y=-\ln(X)\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. If \(X\sim\text{Exp}(1)\), then \(Y=-\ln X\) has CDF \(P(Y\leq y)=P(X\geq e^{-y})=e^{-e^{-y}}\), which is the Gumbel (standard extreme value type I) distribution.
Incorrect. \(-\ln(\text{Exp}(1))\) follows the Gumbel distribution, not normal.
Incorrect. \(-\ln(\text{Exp}(1))\) can take all real values; it cannot be uniform on \((0,1)\).
Incorrect. \(-\ln X\) where \(X\sim\text{Exp}(1)\) is Gumbel-distributed.

questiontext.text: For a bivariate normal \((X,Y)\) with correlation \(\rho\), the conditional distribution of \(Y\mid X=x\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The conditional distribution in a bivariate normal is normal with the given conditional mean (linear regression on \(x\)) and conditional variance \(\sigma_Y^2(1-\rho^2)\).
Incorrect. Conditioning on \(X=x\) shifts the mean and reduces the variance (unless \(\rho=0\)).
Incorrect. The conditional distribution in a bivariate normal is always normal.
Incorrect. The conditional variance is \(\sigma_Y^2(1-\rho^2)\), which is less than \(\sigma_Y^2\) (conditioning reduces uncertainty).

questiontext.text: The median of an Exponential\((\lambda)\) random variable via the quantile function (inverse CDF) is \(F^{-1}(1/2)\). Since \(F(x)=1-e^{-\lambda x}\), we get median \(=\):

feedback.text: Correct. Set \(1-e^{-\lambda m}=1/2\), giving \(e^{-\lambda m}=1/2\), so \(m=\ln2/\lambda\).
Incorrect. \(1/\lambda\) is the mean; median \(=\ln2/\lambda\approx0.693/\lambda\).
Incorrect. Median \(=\ln2/\lambda\).
Incorrect. The support of Exp is \((0,\infty)\); the median is \(\ln2/\lambda>0\).

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet distribution with parameters \((\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_k)\) generalizes the Beta distribution to \(k\) categories. The marginal distribution of \(X_i\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. Each marginal of a Dirichlet is Beta: \(X_i\sim\text{Beta}(\alpha_i, \alpha_0-\alpha_i)\) where \(\alpha_0=\sum_j\alpha_j\).
Incorrect. Marginals are Beta distributions, uniform only when all \(\alpha_i=1\).
Incorrect. The second Beta parameter is the sum of all other \(\alpha_j\), not \(\alpha_i\).
Incorrect. Marginals of Dirichlet are Beta, not exponential.

questiontext.text: The hazard rate (failure rate) of a continuous distribution is \(h(x)=f(x)/S(x)\) where \(S(x)=1-F(x)\). For exponential distribution Exp\((\lambda)\), \(h(x)\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. \(h(x)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}/e^{-\lambda x}=\lambda\) (constant). Constant hazard rate characterizes the exponential distribution and corresponds to its memoryless property.
Incorrect. Increasing hazard rate corresponds to Weibull with \(k>1\), not exponential.
Incorrect. Decreasing hazard rate corresponds to Weibull with \(k<1\).
Incorrect. The hazard rate is \(\lambda\) (the rate parameter), not \(1/\lambda\).

questiontext.text: The truncated normal (truncated to \(x>0\)) has density \(f(x)=\frac{\phi(x)}{1-\Phi(0)}\) for \(x>0\) where \(\phi\) and \(\Phi\) are the standard normal PDF and CDF. For the standard normal, \(\Phi(0)=1/2\), so the truncated mean is:

feedback.text: Correct. For the half-normal (standard normal truncated to positive values): \(E[X|X>0]=\frac{\phi(0)}{1-\Phi(0)}=\frac{1/\sqrt{2\pi}}{1/2}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{2\pi}}=\sqrt{2/\pi}\approx0.798\).
Incorrect. The half-normal has positive mean \(\sqrt{2/\pi}\approx0.798\).
Incorrect. Mean of the half-normal is \(\sqrt{2/\pi}\approx0.798<1\).
Incorrect. Mean is \(\sqrt{2/\pi}\approx0.798\neq\pi/2\approx1.571\).

questiontext.text: The Lévy distribution is a stable distribution with index \(\alpha=1/2\). Its density on \((0,\infty)\) is \(f(x)=\sqrt{c/(2\pi)}\cdot x^{-3/2}e^{-c/(2x)}\). This distribution appears in the context of:

answer.text: First passage times of Brownian motion (the time to first hit a level)

feedback.text: Correct. The first passage time of a standard Brownian motion to level \(c>0\) follows a Lévy distribution, making it fundamental in stochastic processes.

answer.text: The sum of independent exponential random variables

feedback.text: Incorrect. Sums of i.i.d. exponentials give Gamma distributions, not Lévy.

answer.text: The distribution of the sample mean

feedback.text: Incorrect. The sample mean distribution (by CLT) is approximately normal, not Lévy.

answer.text: The distribution of the maximum of normal random variables

feedback.text: Incorrect. The maximum of normals follows a Gumbel (extreme value) distribution, not Lévy.
